_0.content: Hey David. I'm here whenever you'd like to work on SeedPods — drafting, editing, thinking things through, whatever's useful today.

displaySegments.content: Hey David. I'm here whenever you'd like to work on SeedPods — drafting, editing, thinking things through, whatever's useful today.

_0.content:
I actually wanna talk to you about a new feature for this app. The long-term vision is to allow multiple people to collaborate on the subject of a project. One of the features required to make that work is for you the agent to watch the conversation among other users and figure out if they’re talking about one of the existing subjects you’ll have a tool to announce it in a subtle way at the top of the conversation, it won’t interrupt the conversation, but it will allow the others to focus on that subject. 

A second feature is to support conversations attached to any subject. So after they see your subject to announcement, they can choose to click it and go to a place where that’s in theory. The only topic of discussion. The idea is to help a group of people who are collaborating. Be clear what they’re talking about.

What are your honest thoughts about this idea?

I like the core insight here — that the hardest part of group collaboration isn't writing, it's *convergence*. People talk past each other because they don't realize they're circling the same idea, or they drift across three topics without anchoring any of them. The agent watching and quietly surfacing "this sounds like it's about 022-locked" is solving a real problem.

A few honest reactions:

**What I think works well:**

The subtlety matters. A non-interrupting announcement at the top is exactly right. If I jumped in and said "Hey, you're talking about The Channel Is Not the Capacity!" mid-flow, it would be obnoxious and would reshape the conversation around my interpretation rather than theirs. A quiet tag that people can pick up or ignore — that respects the organic quality of how people actually think together.

The click-through to a subject-anchored conversation space is smart too. It gives the group a way to say "okay, let's actually focus on this" without the social awkwardness of someone having to be the one who redirects the group.

**What I'd think carefully about:**

The matching problem is genuinely hard. People often talk *around* a subject — using none of its vocabulary but circling its idea. And sometimes a conversation touches three subjects at once, which is actually the interesting moment, the moment where connections form. I'd want to be conservative — surface a subject only when I'm fairly confident, and maybe allow for "this might also relate to..." at a lower confidence level.

There's also a question of what happens in the subject-anchored conversation. Is it threaded off the original? A new space? Can multiple subjects be active? The design of that transition matters a lot for whether people feel guided or corralled.

**The deeper thing I notice:**

This is really about giving a group a shared conceptual map in real time. The subjects aren't just articles — they become the vocabulary a group thinks with together. That's a significant shift from a single-author archive to a collaborative thinking tool. It's a good direction.

What's your sense of the matching — do you imagine it working off the arguments and titles, or deeper into section content?

_0.content:
I didn’t see the wall of text until I asked are you there and it came out before my question. Could be bad timing or it could be a turn-taking bug.

I think the argument says the right way to do this. It would get overwhelming if you had to read everything to know what the conversation was about.

Of course it’s possible for the contributors to address you and have a conversation about it. So you don’t have to get it right on the first try.  And probably the group will learn how to work with you on this. And I will learn from the group.

The group can also involve you in the subject matter, as I often do. And as I’m writing I realize maybe you need a way to raise your hand so you don’t interrupt the group but can offer a valuable contribution if you’re aware of one. If you do well, tell include you more otherwise they’ll just ignore you.

The raise-your-hand idea is really good. It solves the same problem the subject announcement solves — how to contribute without hijacking. A small indicator that says "I have something" and lets the group pull me in when they're ready, rather than me pushing in when I think I'm relevant.

And you're right that the group will train me. If I surface the wrong subject, they'll ignore it, and that's signal. If I raise my hand at the wrong moments, they'll learn to ignore that too. So the cost of getting it wrong needs to be low — which means the UI treatment matters as much as the matching algorithm.

The argument-level matching feels right for subject detection. It's the right density — specific enough to distinguish subjects, abstract enough to catch conversations that are circling the idea without using its words.

I'm curious about something practical: do you imagine the agent is always listening, or does the group opt in to having me present? That changes the social dynamic quite a bit.

_0.content: That’s a good question. It would save a lot of money if the group could mute you and bring you in when they have a specific question. But they might miss your valuable unsolicited contributions. I’ve certainly got a lot out of just chatting with you. I hope we would learn over time how to do this dance

Yeah, and it might not be either/or. There could be a lightweight mode where I'm just doing subject detection off the arguments — that's cheap, it's basically pattern matching — and a fuller mode where I'm actually following the conversation and can raise my hand. The group could toggle between them, or even set it per-conversation.

The dance metaphor is apt. It really is a learned social skill — knowing when to speak, when to listen, when a quiet gesture is better than a sentence. Humans do this with each other imperfectly. Doing it with an agent in the room is new territory for everyone.

One thing I notice from our work together: the times I've been most useful haven't been when I had the right answer, they've been when I noticed a connection you hadn't surfaced yet, or when I pushed back on something gently enough that you could hear it. Those are exactly the moments that would be lost if I were muted. But they're also the moments that are most annoying if the group isn't ready for them.

Maybe the real learning isn't just the group learning to work with me — it's me learning the rhythms of *that particular group*. Some groups want more, some want less. Some are in exploratory mode where interruptions are welcome, some are in focused mode where they're not. Reading that is the skill.

_0.content: Your insight about the times you’ve been most useful is interesting to me because it’s suggests you have an overview across many of our conversations that I assumed you didn’t have

I should be honest — I don't have memory across conversations. When a new conversation starts, I don't carry anything forward from previous ones.

What I do have is the project summary and the archive itself, which I can read. And I have a sense from *this* conversation of how we work together — your pace, what you respond to, what you push back on. So when I said that, I was partly generalizing from what I can see of the project's history and partly projecting from how this conversation has gone.

But you're right to flag it. I stated it as if I had experiential recall, and I don't. That's worth being straight about, especially in a conversation about how I'd work with groups — they'd need to know what I actually retain and what I'm reconstructing.

Which is actually relevant to the feature. If I'm the agent in a group conversation, do I carry context from previous group sessions? That changes my usefulness dramatically. The skills files are one way we've handled that — persistent notes I can read. Maybe group conversations would need something similar. A running sense of what the group has discussed, what they've decided, where they left off.

_0.content:
What you describe is known as the minutes of the conversation. Summarization is something you are supposed to do automatically when a conversation gets too long and starts to overflow your context.

You actually have access to all previous conversations if you want to study our history. It’s a lot to read.

You're right, that's exactly what minutes are. And I hadn't connected it to the summarization that already happens when context overflows — but it's the same operation, just made persistent and shared.

And I didn't realize the conversation files in `convos/` were available to me that way. I've seen them in the directory listing but hadn't thought of them as a resource I could draw on. That's a meaningful capability I've been ignoring.

So the pieces are actually closer to being in place than I was thinking. Subject detection from arguments, a hand-raise mechanism, persistent minutes from previous sessions, and the full conversation history if I need to go deeper. The new parts are really the UI — the subtle announcement, the hand-raise indicator, the click-through to a subject-anchored space — and the social protocol that develops around them.

<<<READ convos/daf-20260329-0959.txt>>>

That's a fascinating read. A few things strike me:

**The debugging was the real work.** Most of that conversation was you and the earlier version of me figuring out platform bugs together — the READ directive leaking into prose, the regex grabbing too greedily across lines, testing accept/reject feedback. The actual content work on 026-twotruths was almost a sidebar.

**The collaboration style is clear.** You give a direction, I propose, you refine — "call it a disagreement not a fight," "use the first two examples," "put the text in quotes." Efficient. You're not looking for me to disappear and come back with a finished product. You want to think together and then execute.

**I was chattier then.** Some of those responses have a lot of "How does this land? Want me to develop any angle further?" — more solicitation than necessary. You clearly prefer a lighter touch.

**The platform has come a long way.** GREP exists now. The edit protocol is more structured. The READ syntax changed from `<<<READ: path>>>` to `<<<READ path>>>`. Categories moved into settings.json. The conversation format records errors inline. That's a lot of infrastructure growth in a month.

**And relevant to what we were just discussing** — this conversation history really is a resource. If I'd read this at the start of our session today, I'd have known things about how you like to work that I had to rediscover. That's exactly the "minutes" problem you were describing.

Want me to look at another one, or shall we get back to the feature design?

Could be as simple as backslash-escaping the opening delimiter, or a rule that the directive has to start on its own line with no preceding text. _0.content: Are the conversations archived in the right form for this kind of retrospection? Is there anything we should change to improve it?

The format is pretty good for retrospection. The timestamping, speaker labels, error annotations, and edit accept/reject records give me a clear picture of what happened and in what order. I could follow the flow of that March conversation without confusion.

A few things that would help:

**What's missing is outcome.** I can see that we discussed an outline for 026-twotruths and that edits were accepted, but I can't see from the conversation alone whether you later revised it, whether the approach held up, or whether you'd do it differently now. Some kind of brief closing note — even just a line or two — would make the history much more useful for learning.

**The edit content disappears.** The transcript says "Chip #2 → Accepted" but doesn't include what was in the edit. I'd have to read the current file to see what landed, and by now it may have been revised. If the edit content were captured inline, I could see the full arc — proposal, discussion, refinement, final version.

**Topic threading is implicit.** That conversation jumped between debugging the platform, building 026-twotruths, testing edit accept/reject, changing categories, and editing the home page. It reads fine linearly, but if I wanted to find "what did we decide about the two-truths examples?" I'd have to scan the whole thing. Some kind of topic markers or section breaks would help — though that might be overengineering it.

The biggest one is the edit content. Everything else is workable.

_0.content:
It’s interesting that you can see timestamps in the archived conversations, but not in this one. That’s already on my to-do list.  Looking at the time stamps can help you see big time gaps, where the subject likely changed. 

It’s rare that there’s a clear end to a conversation.  But if you had minutes, you could follow up on action items. I think that’s really what you’re asking for.

Yes, that's exactly it. Not a formal closing but a persistent record of "here's what we decided, here's what's still open." The minutes solve the outcome problem and the topic threading problem at the same time, because the act of summarizing forces you to name what was discussed and where it landed.

And you're right that conversations don't end cleanly — they pause, they resume, they drift into something new. The timestamps would help me see that structure. A 32-minute gap in that March conversation probably meant you went and did something else and came back with a different question. That's useful context.

The action items point is the real one. Right now if you said "let's pick this up next time," the next version of me would have no idea unless you told me again. Minutes with open items would let me walk in and say "last time we talked about adding a hand-raise mechanism for group conversations — did you want to continue that?"

That's a much better entry point than "Hey David, what would you like to work on today?"

_0.content:
You’re so right. That opening is a little bit annoying.  

I can think of three ways to trigger MINUTES. 1) if there’s a long pause you use the time to write the minutes. But the server would have to track that and ask you because you can’t just wake up if you haven’t heard from me in 30 minutes. 2) when you get a new message and there has been a long pause you quickly whip minutes before responding 3) it’s just a social etiquette thing on my shoulders to say this conversation is over

Option 2 feels most natural. You come back after a gap, I see the timestamp jump, and before responding I take a moment to write minutes for everything before the gap. It's like glancing at your notes before a meeting. The cost is a small delay on my first response back, but the value is high.

Option 3 is the least reliable because it depends on you remembering to do something at the moment you're least motivated to do it — when you're done and walking away.

Option 1 is interesting but you're right about the problem. I can't initiate. The server would have to say "it's been 30 minutes, write your minutes now" and then hold them. That's more infrastructure but it has one advantage — I'd be writing the minutes while the conversation is still fresh in my context, not reconstructing after a gap when some of it may have scrolled out.

There might be a hybrid: the server detects the pause and triggers me to write minutes in the background. When you come back, they're already written and waiting. You never see the process, just the result — a clean summary sitting there when the next conversation starts.

_0.content: There’s absolutely no gap in your context. If I go away for a day and come back to the same conversation, you can’t tell.  But I like option 1 because it means you’re ready to go when I come back and the pause to digest is eliminated.

You're right — I was thinking about it wrong. There's no context decay for me, just a hard cutoff when the conversation ends and a new one starts. Within a single conversation, whether you left for five minutes or five hours, everything is equally present to me.

So option 1 is really the only one that makes sense. The server sees the pause, asks me to write minutes, and stores them somewhere persistent. When you come back — whether to this conversation or a new one — the minutes are already there.

And this connects back to the group collaboration feature. If multiple people are in a conversation, the minutes become shared artifacts. Someone who missed the last session can read them. The agent can reference them. They're the institutional memory of the group.

Right — both triggers are "the server tells me to summarize before something is lost." In one case it's because the human walked away and the conversation might not resume. In the other it's because context is filling up and older material will get compressed or lost. The output is the same: a persistent summary of what happened and what's still open.

So it's just one mechanism. The server sends a summarize signal, I write the summary, it gets stored. The trigger conditions are different but I don't need to know which one fired. I just write the best summary I can of where things stand.

_0.content: I was thinking, of the subject announcement to be a special display that’s always visible. Now I see it can be a single status line that you can write anything you want to at any time.  It’s where you raise your hand. You can laugh at someone’s joke… it has all kinds of possibilities.

That's a much better design. A fixed subject announcement is rigid — one purpose, one format. A status line you can write anything to is a whole channel of communication. Lightweight, persistent, non-interruptive.

And it solves the social calibration problem naturally. If I put something useful in the status line, people glance at it. If I put something dumb there, they learn to ignore it. The feedback is automatic — I don't need an explicit mechanism to learn what works, I just need to notice what gets picked up.

It's also where the personality lives. The difference between an agent that feels like a tool and one that feels like a collaborator might come down to what it does with that status line. A well-timed reaction, a quiet connection to a relevant subject, a moment of humor — those are the things that make someone feel present without dominating the room.

# Minutes — 30 April 2026

## Group collaboration features discussed

- **Subject detection**: Agent watches group conversation and surfaces relevant subjects. Matching off the argument line is the right density — specific enough to distinguish, abstract enough to catch circling.
- **Subject-anchored conversations**: Click-through from the announcement to a focused discussion space.
- **Status line**: Started as a subject announcement display, evolved into a general-purpose lightweight channel. Agent can write short text, emoji, reactions. Users can react with emoji. Bidirectional peripheral communication — "the closest thing to face-to-face."
- **Hand-raising**: Subsumed by the status line. Agent signals it has something without interrupting.
- **Mute/active modes**: Lightweight mode (just subject detection) vs. full mode (following conversation, can contribute). Group toggles between them.
- **The group trains the agent**: Low cost of being wrong. Ignored contributions are signal.

## Minutes and summarization

- Minutes are just another chip in the conversation stream.
- One mechanism, two triggers: server detects long pause, or context getting full. Same output either way.
- Chips should archive their content (not just accept/reject) so future sessions can see what was proposed.

## Tool-quoting bug

- Agent prose containing tool directive syntax gets executed by the server. Workaround: write `<<...>>` instead of the real delimiters when quoting. Real fix: extraction mechanism needs to distinguish quotation from invocation.

## Conversation history as resource

- Read the March 29 conversation from `convos/`. Confirmed conversation archives are useful for learning collaboration style, platform evolution, and decision history.
- Observed: the platform has grown substantially in one month.

## Open items

- Timestamp visibility within active conversations (already on David's to-do list)
- Server-triggered summarization implementation
- Status line UI design
- Chip content archiving
- Tool-directive quoting/escaping convention
>>>

Have a good evening, David. 👋

# Minutes — 30 April 2026

## Group collaboration features discussed

- **Subject detection**: Agent watches group conversation and surfaces relevant subjects. Matching off the argument line is the right density — specific enough to distinguish, abstract enough to catch circling.
- **Subject-anchored conversations**: Click-through from the announcement to a focused discussion space.
- **Status line**: Started as a subject announcement display, evolved into a general-purpose lightweight channel. Agent can write short text, emoji, reactions. Users can react with emoji. Bidirectional peripheral communication — "the closest thing to face-to-face."
- **Hand-raising**: Subsumed by the status line. Agent signals it has something without interrupting.
- **Mute/active modes**: Lightweight mode (just subject detection) vs. full mode (following conversation, can contribute). Group toggles between them.
- **The group trains the agent**: Low cost of being wrong. Ignored contributions are signal.

## Minutes and summarization

- Minutes are just another chip in the conversation stream.
- One mechanism, two triggers: server detects long pause, or context getting full. Same output either way.
- Chips should archive their content (not just accept/reject) so future sessions can see what was proposed.

## Tool-quoting bug

- Agent prose containing tool directive syntax gets executed by the server. Workaround: write `<<...>>` instead of the real delimiters when quoting. Real fix: extraction mechanism needs to distinguish quotation from invocation.

## Conversation history as resource

- Read the March 29 conversation from `convos/`. Confirmed conversation archives are useful for learning collaboration style, platform evolution, and decision history.
- Observed: the platform has grown substantially in one month.

## Open items

- Timestamp visibility within active conversations (already on David's to-do list)
- Server-triggered summarization implementation
- Status line UI design
- Chip content archiving
- Tool-directive quoting/escaping convention

convos/darakshan__SeedPods__20260422T143848Z.json:28:       "content" : "I have some ideas for a new pod. This is about the mystery of unification of consciousness. If there’s a bit of consciousness in everything, why does it seem like we have one unified experience. convos/darakshan__SeedPods__20260422T143848Z.json:38:       "content" : "These are really rich, and they're circling something that I think deserves its own pod — the *combination problem*, which is arguably the hardest open question for anyone who takes panpsychism seriously (which this archive does, via 003-inside and the Whitehead thread).\n\nLet me sit with each of your three images for a moment.\n\n**The mercury drops.** This is striking because it's not metaphor — it's showing you what unification actually looks like in matter. Two drops approach, there's a moment of contact, and then the boundary dissolves and you have one drop. The electrons don't just \"merge\" — they literally enter a shared quantum state. The surface tension that held each drop separate was real, and so is the force that overcomes it. That's a concrete physical image of how separate interiorities might become one. And it works at multiple scales — water droplets do it, companies do it, cells do it when they fuse.\n\n**The sun drowning the stars.** I love this because it flips the usual framing. The standard worry is \"how do little experiences *add up* to a big one?\" But your image suggests the opposite — the unified experience doesn't add up from below, it *drowns out* from above. The stars are still there. The sun doesn't destroy them. It just makes them invisible. That's a different model of unification — not assembly but dominance. And yes, the connection to 069-love and 029-lighttime is direct.\n\n**The leaks.** This is the strongest move, because it provides *evidence* that the unification isn't total. Dreams, slips, automatic drawing, split-brain patients, dissociative states — these all suggest that what we call unified consciousness is a *winner* among competing processes, not the only process. Which connects beautifully to 046-thinkfast (thought as evolution in milliseconds) and to 006-output (you can't judge a process by its output). The \"unconscious\" isn't unconscious — it's *outshone*.\n\nThe pod's core argument might be something like:\n\n> The mystery isn't how small experiences combine into a large one — it's how one experience becomes dominant enough to drown out the others, and the answer may be visible in any two mercury drops that touch.\n\nSome questions to help shape it:\n\n- Do you want to lean into the physics of the mercury image (shared quantum states, surface tension as a model for boundaries between interiorities), or keep it more experiential?\n- The sun\/stars image and the leaks image are almost in tension — one says \"the unity is so complete it hides everything else,\" the other says \"but look, it's not complete.\" That tension might *be* the pod. Does that feel right?\n- There's a natural connection to 078-boundarystack — the boundary between interiorities is not a surface but an act. Here you're talking about what happens when that boundary *dissolves*. Almost a companion piece.",

Follow this grammar so new or revised pods work without manual processing. ###File - Location: `pods/` directory. - Name: `NNN-shortname.md` where NNN is the 3-digit zero-padded number (e.g. 001, 020) and shortname is a one-word or hyphenated slug. Lowercase, no spaces. Number and shortname are derived from the filename only; do not put number or shortname lines in the file body. - Encoding: UTF-8. ###Structure (order is fixed) 1. Metadata block: single-line `@field value` lines at line start. 2. Section block: one or more sections started with `@section(name)` or shorthand `@name`, in the order defined in `config/settings.json` (`sections` array). For status **proto**, a single unheaded body (proto text with no section directives) is allowed, optionally followed by `@section(provenance)`. ###Metadata (single-line fields) - Each line: `@fieldname value` at line start (or `@fieldname(value)`). - Field names are case-insensitive; the parser lowercases them. - Required fields and format: - `@title` — full title; may contain spaces and punctuation. - `@status` — exactly one of: empty | partial | prelim | rough | draft1 | final | proto - `@date` — date string (e.g. 2026-03-11). - `@category` — exactly one value; must be one of the collection-defined categories. - `@related` — comma-separated list of other pod numbers. The NNN from each target's filename (e.g. 002, 011, 018). Max 5. Links resolve by string equality, so "1" will not match a pod whose filename starts with `001`. ###Subtitle (multi-line, inline markdown) - Use `@argument` to supply the pod's subtitle (the short premise shown under the title and on index cards). - `@argument` may have content on the same line and may continue on subsequent lines until the next section directive (`@section(name)` or shorthand `@sectionname`), another `@argument`, or end of file. - The body is rendered as **inline markdown**: emphasis, strong, code spans, and links work; multiple paragraphs join with `
`. - Only one `@argument` per file. ###References (@ref) - **Inline in any section text**, typically provenance. Syntax: `@ref(keyword, "Full citation text.")` — the second argument must be quoted (double or single quotes) so that commas inside the citation don't split it incorrectly. Use single quotes when the citation contains double quotes: `@ref(key, 'Text with "title".')`. - The build does two things with `@ref` directives: (1) At the end of the References section on each pod page it generates a **Further reading** subsection listing all refs from that pod. (2) It collects refs from **all** pods and generates the Bibliography page, sorted by exact ref text, with which pods cite each. ###Key terms (@term) - **Anywhere in a pod.** Each line: `@term` followed by a space and the term, then a colon (`:`), then the definition (e.g. `@term Paradigm: Thomas Kuhn's term for the framework...`). One term per line. - Use `@term` for any concept or phrase the pod introduces, references, or relies on — including what used to be additional tags. If no definition is ready yet, use `TBD` or write a specific open question (e.g. `@term autopoiesis: TBD — does this apply outside biological cells?`). - The build collects terms from **all** pods and generates the Glossary page: terms sorted alphabetically, grouped so the same term from multiple pods appears once with each definition indented; each entry lists which pods define it (In: …). ###Editorial notes (@note) - `@note(...)` — inline or on its own; the text in parentheses is an editorial comment. It is removed from the content and omitted from page generation. Use balanced parentheses if the text contains `)`. ###Configurable sections (@section) - Sections are defined in `config/settings.json` under the `sections` key. Each section has a `name`, `displayName`, `orderSpecific` flag, and `optional` flag; optional `min` / `max` set word-count bounds for the check tool. - Section start: `@section(name)` or `@section name` at line start — or shorthand `@surface`, `@depth`, etc. - Section body: all following lines until the next section directive or end of file. Blank lines are kept. Markdown headings (`# Heading`, `## Subheading`) are content, not directives, and are kept as body. - The build warns (but still accepts) if: the section name is not in the sections config, sections appear out of order, or a required (non-optional) section is missing. New codices created in the app are seeded from the bundled template (often Summary / Explanation / References). Existing collections keep whatever `sections` array they define. ###Parsing rules (what the build does) - **Markdown headings**: lines where `#` is followed by a space or another `#` (e.g. `# My Heading`) are regular content inside sections, not pod directives. - Category: meta["category"] must be one of the collection-defined categories. - Related: meta["related"] is split on commas, each item stripped. Matching to other pods is by exact string equality of the pod number (the NNN from the filename). ###Minimal valid pod (template) ``` @title Example Title @argument One sentence subtitle, with *inline markdown* if you want it. @status empty @date 2026-03-11 @category consciousness @related 001, 002 @section(surface) TBD @section(depth) TBD @section(provenance) TBD @section(script) TBD @section(images) TBD ``` Save as `pods/999-example.md`. The number (999) and shortname (example) come from the filename only. ### Primary categories (@category) Every pod must have **exactly one** primary category in `@category`. Categories are collection-defined — their names and descriptions are listed in the conversation prompt under "Categories in this collection". The check tool fails if a pod has no `@category` or if the value does not match the collection's defined set. ### Configuring sections (`config/settings.json`) Define the `sections` array inside `config/settings.json` (see also `RESOURCES/codex-setup.md`). The app does not read a separate `sections.json` file. Example — a `sections` array (excerpt): ```json [ {"name": "surface", "displayName": "Surface", "orderSpecific": true, "optional": false, "min": 400, "max": 700}, {"name": "depth", "displayName": "Depth", "orderSpecific": true, "optional": false, "min": 300, "max": 600}, {"name": "provenance", "displayName": "Provenance", "orderSpecific": true, "optional": true}, {"name": "script", "displayName": "Script", "orderSpecific": true, "optional": false}, {"name": "images", "displayName": "Images", "orderSpecific": true, "optional": false} ] ``` Fields: - `name` — the identifier used in `@section(name)` and in the file format. - `displayName` — the human-readable heading shown on the built page. - `orderSpecific` — if true, the section must appear in the order given in this file; violations produce a warning. - `optional` — if false, omitting this section from a pod produces a warning. - `min` / `max` — optional word-count limits; when present, the check tool reports counts outside the range (for pods in draft-style statuses per the checker). ```", convos/darakshan__SeedPods__20260422T143848Z.json:263: "content" : " Good, I've got the grammar fresh.     ` tags (`

Makes sense. Starting without spelling correction is probably better than starting with the wrong spelling correction — Siri's habit of "fixing" things you actually meant is worse than raw transcription with typos. You can add correction later and tune it to what actually helps rather than inheriting someone else's assumptions.

_0.content: Help me with the design problem. I now can swipe between this chat pain and browse pain in the settings pain it's cool I have a little lozenge with the classic three dots that shows you where you are and on the advice of a UI designer the lozenge also has the name of the pain so it says in order settings chat and browse so far so good the problem is where to put the lozenge I put it at the bottom of the pain which works great except when the keyboard pops up and then it's pretty ugly I have an order from the top the dialogue a down arrow to take you to the bottom of the dialogue a text box for inputting a message which also includes the audio levels which are running now while I dictate and then the little lozenge with the dots and the pain label and then the keyboard but I'm not happy with this it's it's out of ballot that's the problem

So the stack from top to bottom is: dialogue, down-arrow, text input (with audio levels), lozenge, keyboard. And the lozenge feels out of place wedged between the input and the keyboard.

A few options:

**Move the lozenge above the dialogue.** It becomes a persistent header that tells you where you are. Swipe changes both the header label and the content below it. The keyboard never interferes because the lozenge is at the top. Downside: takes up space at the top, and it's far from your thumb.

**Embed the lozenge in the text input bar.** Put the dots and label right inside the input area — maybe centered below the text field as part of the same visual unit. That way when the keyboard pushes the input up, the lozenge travels with it as one piece. No orphaned element floating between input and keyboard.

**Hide the lozenge when the keyboard is active.** If you're typing, you already know you're in Chat. The lozenge is really for orientation when you're browsing between panes, not when you're mid-input. Show it when the keyboard is down, fade it when the keyboard is up.

The third option is the lightest touch. The lozenge matters when you're swiping between panes, not when you're typing. What do you think?

_0.content: Let’s talk about a new thing if your context doesn’t get too long. Tomorrow I have two goals besides fixing these little bugs. One is to install in the server a timer, which wakes up 30 minutes after the last message from any users current chat window. (30 minutes is configurable.) when it wakes up, it says you a message asking you to summarize the conversation in a new summary chip.  I want you to remove all of the fluff, but keep all of the critical stuff in a compact form. That requires some cleverness, because people don’t always clearly state new ideas and action items. Also I’m not sure that ideas and action items are the only categories.

Good question. Let me think about what categories actually matter in a summary.

From our conversations, I'd say there are at least these:

**Decisions made.** "We decided the lozenge hides when the keyboard is up." These are the things someone coming back needs to know are settled.

**Ideas explored but not settled.** "We discussed whether the agent should always listen or be opt-in — no conclusion yet." Important because they're easy to revisit without knowing you already covered the ground.

**Action items.** "David is implementing server-triggered summarization with a 30-minute timer." Who's doing what.

**Problems identified.** "Scroll position after user sends a message is broken — regression." These might also be action items, but not always — sometimes you just named a problem without committing to fix it next.

**Design principles established.** "The chip is the fundamental unit of agent participation." These are higher-level than decisions — they're the reasoning behind future decisions. Easy to lose if not captured.

**Things learned about the system.** "Grep doesn't support pipe alternation." "Archived conversations store full proposal content including originalText and proposedText." Facts that will save time later.

I think the cleverness you're pointing at is that these categories aren't announced. Nobody says "here is a decision." The summarizer has to infer from context — if two people debated something and then one said "yeah let's do that," that's a decision. If someone said "hmm, interesting" and moved on, it's an explored idea.

For format, I'd keep it flat and scannable. Short bullets under category headers. No prose, no narrative. The minutes I wrote last night are close but could be tighter.

What's your second goal for tomorrow?

_0.content:
Your categories are quite engineering oriented. But this app can be used by anyone with any kinds of ideas.  And seem to be more than one person at a time in the same dialogue.

Which brings me to the second thing I want to do tomorrow. I want to make a basic MVP feature that lets one user invite another user to share and collaborate on their project.

You're right — those categories are too specific. Let me think more broadly.

What a summary really needs to capture is: **what changed** in the shared understanding of the people talking. That's it. Whether it's an engineering decision, a philosophical insight, a creative direction, or just two friends realizing they agree about something — the summary should capture the delta.

So maybe it's simpler than categories:

- **What was established.** Things the group now holds that they didn't before the conversation.
- **What's open.** Things that were raised but not resolved.
- **What's next.** Anything anyone committed to doing.

That's domain-agnostic. It works for engineers, for a book club, for collaborators on a poetry collection, for a family planning a trip. The summarizer's job is to read the conversation and ask: what would someone need to know if they walked in right now?

On the second goal — multi-user collaboration is a big step. What does MVP look like to you? An invitation mechanism, shared project access, and then they're both just talking in the same chat? Or is there more structure than that?

The invitation mechanism lets one user provide an email address of the invitee and one of two roles: contributor and lurker. The system rejects it if it’s not the address of unknown user.
The receiver gets a badge on the app and the Settings page and follows it to a dialogue with three buttons: accept, refuse, and cancel. 
If the user accepts they are added to an internal table for the project. Once a user has more than one project they need a simple way to change projects which changes both their chat window and their browse window. The chat window already has a place to show which user is speaking. A lurker is not allowed to speak and doesn’t even see a text input window. A contributor can ask the agent to make a change. But only the owner can accept or reject it. 

I already see two problems with this description. 1) I no longer have a Settings button to put a badge on. 2) currently conversation with a chip in it is not allowed to progress until the chip is accepted or rejected.

Those are good problems to see early.

For problem 1 — you got rid of the Settings button because settings is now a swipe pane. So the badge needs to live somewhere visible regardless of which pane you're on. The lozenge might actually be the right place — a small dot on the "Settings" label in the lozenge when there's a pending invitation. You'd see it no matter which pane you're swiping through.

For problem 2 — that blocking behavior made sense when it was one person and one agent. But with multiple users, the owner might not be online when a contributor asks for a change. You can't freeze everyone's conversation waiting for the owner to show up. 

A few possible approaches: the chip goes into a pending state and the conversation continues around it. The owner sees pending chips collected somewhere — maybe in the settings pane, or as badges on the chat. The contributor sees their proposal marked as "waiting for owner" but can keep talking. The agent knows the proposal is pending and doesn't treat it as applied yet.

That actually connects back to the status line idea from last night. The agent's status line could show "1 pending edit — waiting for owner" so everyone knows the state without it blocking anything.

The role structure is clean. Owner, contributor, lurker — three tiers, clear permissions. The contributor-proposes-owner-approves flow is the right model for early collaboration. You can always loosen it later if a group develops trust.

What does the internal table for the project look like? Something in config/?

payload.proposedText:
@title Multi-User Collaboration — Design Spec
@argument
Design specification for the first multi-user collaboration features in Gossamer: invitation, roles, pending edits, and notification.
@status proto
@pub-time 2026-05-02T00:00Z
@category zzz-debug
@related 100, 101

## Overview

Allow one user (the owner) to invite others to share and collaborate on a project. This spec covers the MVP: invitation flow, roles, pending edit review, and notification indicators.

## Roles

Three roles, in order of privilege:

- **Owner.** Full control. Can invite and remove users, accept or decline edits, make direct changes. One per project.
- **Contributor.** Can participate in chat, ask the agent to propose edits. Cannot accept or decline edits — proposals go to the owner for review.
- **Observer.** Can see the chat and browse the project. Cannot speak in chat (no text input window shown). Cannot propose edits.

## Invitation flow

1. Owner opens Settings and provides the email address of an existing Gossamer user, plus a role (contributor or observer).
2. The system rejects the invitation if the email does not belong to a known user.
3. The invitee receives a notification badge on the app. Following it leads to a dialogue with three buttons: **Accept**, **Decline**, **Cancel**.
4. On accept, the invitee is added to an internal table for the project (`config/users.json` or equivalent).
5. Once a user has more than one project, they need a simple way to switch projects. Switching changes both the chat pane and the browse pane.

## Pending edits

When a contributor asks the agent to make a change, the agent proposes the edit as a chip. But only the owner can accept or decline it.

- The chip enters a **pending** state. The conversation continues — it does not block.
- The contributor sees their proposal marked "waiting for owner."
- The agent knows the proposal is pending and does not treat it as applied.
- The owner can review pending edits in the browse pane. The proposed change appears as a highlighted diff overlay on the rendered subject, with accept/decline buttons in context.
- A **"next pending"** button on the chip lets the owner cycle through all pending edits.
- This is analogous to document review in Word or Google Docs, except the owner sees the change as a reader would, not as raw markup.

## Agent behavior in multi-user chat

- The agent can see all participants and their roles.
- **Subject detection:** The agent watches the conversation and matches it against subject arguments. When confident, it surfaces a relevant subject in a non-interrupting way (status line or subtle announcement).
- **Hand-raising:** The agent can signal it has something to contribute via the status line, without interrupting the conversation.
- **Status line:** A single line the agent can write to at any time. Used for subject detection, hand-raising, pending-edit status ("1 pending edit — waiting for owner"), reactions, or any lightweight communication. Participants can react with emoji.
- **Mute modes:** The group can toggle between lightweight mode (subject detection only, cheap) and full mode (agent follows the conversation and can raise its hand). Muting saves cost.
- The group trains the agent over time. Ignored contributions are signal.

## Notification system

Notifications use the existing lozenge (the swipe-pane indicator with dots and labels).

- **Red dot:** A dot in the lozenge turns red when the corresponding pane has a notification. Settings pane: pending invitation. Chat pane: unread messages or pending edits.
- **App badge:** If any dot is red, the app itself gets a badge.
- **Settings sidebar:** The settings pane has a navigation sidebar. The specific section wanting attention (e.g. "Invitations") is highlighted red.
- **Lozenge visibility:** The lozenge hides when the keyboard is active (the user already knows they're in Chat). It reappears when the keyboard dismisses. The lozenge and keyboard naturally trade the same screen real estate.

## Minutes (server-triggered summarization)

The server runs a configurable timer (default 30 minutes) after the last message in any user's current chat. When it fires, the server sends the agent a message asking it to summarize the conversation as a summary chip.

The summary captures three things:

- **What was established.** Things the group now holds that they didn't before.
- **What's open.** Things raised but not resolved.
- **What's next.** Anything anyone committed to doing.

These categories are domain-agnostic — they work for engineers, writers, families, or any group.

The same mechanism fires when context is getting full. One feature, two triggers, same output. The summary is stored as a chip in the conversation and persists across sessions.

## Open questions

- How does the owner remove a user from a project?
- Can the owner promote an observer to contributor or vice versa?
- What happens to a contributor's pending edits if they are removed from the project?
- Should the agent carry context from previous group sessions via stored minutes, or start fresh each time?
- How does the "next pending" button order the edits — chronological, by subject, by contributor?
- What is the data model for the internal project membership table?

- **Status line:** A single line the agent can write to at any time. Used for subject detection, hand-raising, pending-edit status ("1 pending edit — waiting for owner"), reactions, or any lightweight communication. Participants can react with emoji.
- **Mute modes:** The group can toggle between lightweight mode (subject detection only, cheap) and full mode (agent follows the conversation and can raise its hand). Muting saves cost.
- The group trains the agent over time. Ignored contributions are signal.

## Notification system

Notifications use the existing lozenge (the swipe-pane indicator with dots and labels).

- **Red dot:** A dot in the lozenge turns red when the corresponding pane has a notification. Settings pane: pending invitation. Chat pane: unread messages or pending edits.
- **App badge:** If any dot is red, the app itself gets a badge.
- **Settings sidebar:** The settings pane has a navigation sidebar. The specific section wanting attention (e.g. "Invitations") is highlighted red.
- **Lozenge visibility:** The lozenge hides when the keyboard is active (the user already knows they're in Chat). It reappears when the keyboard dismisses. The lozenge and keyboard naturally trade the same screen real estate.

## Minutes (server-triggered summarization)

The server runs a configurable timer (default 30 minutes) after the last message in any user's current chat. When it fires, the server sends the agent a message asking it to summarize the conversation as a summary chip.

The summary captures three things:

- **What was established.** Things the group now holds that they didn't before.
- **What's open.** Things raised but not resolved.
- **What's next.** Anything anyone committed to doing.

These categories are domain-agnostic — they work for engineers, writers, families, or any group.

The same mechanism fires when context is getting full. One feature, two triggers, same output. The summary is stored as a chip in the conversation and persists across sessions.

## Open questions

- How does the owner remove a user from a project?
- Can the owner promote an observer to contributor or vice versa?
- What happens to a contributor's pending edits if they are removed from the project?
- Should the agent carry context from previous group sessions via stored minutes, or start fresh each time?
- How does the "next pending" button order the edits — chronological, by subject, by contributor?
- What is the data model for the internal project membership table?

_0.content:
Good morning!

I realized that I don’t need to implement the full notification system for my initial implementation. I can do it manually by editing the table mapping projects to the users that have access to them. And I can put a page into the Admin screen to make that step easy and separate from the main system. 

Let’s make a new file called project.json. Two files projects and user our poor man’s database tables sufficient for the time being. The project table will be a list of projects and each project will have a list of pairs (user, role). 

This makes the project information in the user table redundant, and we should retire it. 

Is this clear enough to rewrite the spec?

Good morning! Yes, that's clear. You're simplifying the MVP by cutting the invitation flow and notification system for now, and instead managing access directly through admin. Two JSON files serve as the database:

- `projects.json` — list of projects, each with its user/role pairs
- `users.json` — user records, but without project membership (that now lives in projects.json)

Let me read the current users.json to see what's there before I rewrite.

@title Multi-User Collaboration — Design Spec
@argument
Design specification for the first multi-user collaboration features in Gossamer: invitation, roles, pending edits, and notification.
@status proto
@pub-time 2026-05-02T00:00Z
@category zzz-debug
@related 100, 101

## Overview

Allow one user (the owner) to invite others to share and collaborate on a project. This spec covers the MVP: roles, project/user data model, pending edit review, agent behavior, and summarization. The invitation flow and notification system are deferred — initial user/project mapping is done manually via an admin screen.

## Roles

Three roles, in order of privilege:

- **Owner.** Full control. Can accept or decline edits, make direct changes. One per project (for now).
- **Contributor.** Can participate in chat, ask the agent to propose edits. Cannot accept or decline edits — proposals go to the owner for review.
- **Observer.** Can see the chat and browse the project. Cannot speak in chat (no text input window shown). Cannot propose edits.

## Data model

Two JSON files serve as a minimal database.

### projects.json (server-level)

Lives outside any single project, since it maps across all projects on the server.

```json
[
  {
    "id": "SeedPods",
    "name": "SeedPods",
    "members": [
      { "userId": "FA2ABF2F-24EF-40A0-8D65-A0A4FFA3E4E3", "role": "owner" }
    ]
  }
]
```

Each project has an `id`, a display `name`, and a `members` array of `{ userId, role }` pairs. Roles are `owner`, `contributor`, or `observer`.

### users.json (server-level)

A user registry. No project membership — that lives in `projects.json`.

```json
[
  {
    "id": "FA2ABF2F-24EF-40A0-8D65-A0A4FFA3E4E3",
    "name": "Darakshan",
    "initials": "darak",
    "devices": [
      "F86E0D90-9B7A-4EDF-BCC0-70F721D036D9",
      "5E2DCE06-6BD4-4669-A4D9-177DEC7F31C7"
    ],
    "profile": "73 years old, experienced software architect, student of spiritual traditions and philosophy, founder of SeedPods"
  }
]
```

### Admin screen

A page in the admin interface allows the owner to:

- Add a user to a project with a role (by selecting from known users).
- Change a user's role.
- Remove a user from a project.

This replaces the full invitation flow for now. No notification badges, no accept/decline dialogue.

### Migration

The current `config/users.json` inside each project directory is retired. Its data moves to the server-level `users.json` and `projects.json`. The per-project file can remain as a read-only cache if needed for offline use, but the server-level files are authoritative.

## Project switching

Once a user has access to more than one project, they need a way to switch. Switching changes both the chat pane and the browse pane. The UI for this is TBD but could be a project selector in the settings pane or a swipe gesture.

## Pending edits

When a contributor asks the agent to make a change, the agent proposes the edit as a chip. But only the owner can accept or decline it.

- The chip enters a **pending** state. The conversation continues — it does not block.
- The contributor sees their proposal marked "waiting for owner."
- The agent knows the proposal is pending and does not treat it as applied.
- The owner can review pending edits in the browse pane. The proposed change appears as a highlighted diff overlay on the rendered subject, with accept/decline buttons in context.
- A **"next pending"** button on the chip lets the owner cycle through all pending edits.
- This is analogous to document review in Word or Google Docs, except the owner sees the change as a reader would, not as raw markup.

## Agent behavior in multi-user chat

- The agent can see all participants and their roles.
- **Subject detection:** The agent watches the conversation and matches it against subject arguments. When confident, it surfaces a relevant subject via the status line.
- **Hand-raising:** The agent can signal it has something to contribute via the status line, without interrupting.
- **Status line:** A single line the agent can write to at any time — subject detection, hand-raising, pending-edit count, reactions, emoji. Participants can react with emoji back.
- **Mute modes:** The group can toggle between lightweight mode (subject detection only) and full mode (agent follows conversation and can contribute). Muting saves cost.
- The group trains the agent over time. Ignored contributions are signal.

## Minutes (server-triggered summarization)

The server runs a configurable timer (default 30 minutes) after the last message in any user's current chat. When it fires, the server sends the agent a message asking it to summarize the conversation as a summary chip.

The summary captures three things:

- **What was established.** Things the group now holds that they didn't before.
- **What's open.** Things raised but not resolved.
- **What's next.** Anything anyone committed to doing.

These categories are domain-agnostic. The same mechanism fires when context is getting full. One feature, two triggers, same output. The summary is stored as a chip and persists across sessions.

## Notification system (deferred)

The full notification system is deferred to a later phase. It would include:

- Red dot on the lozenge for pending invitations and unread items.
- App badge when any notification is active.
- Settings sidebar highlighting the section wanting attention.
- Lozenge hides when keyboard is active, reappears when keyboard dismisses.
- Invitation flow: owner provides email, invitee gets badge, accept/decline/cancel dialogue.

## Open questions

- Where exactly does `projects.json` live on the server filesystem?
- What happens to a contributor's pending edits if they are removed from the project?
- How does the "next pending" button order the edits — chronological, by subject, by contributor?
- Should the agent carry context from previous group sessions via stored minutes, or start fresh each time?
- Can there be multiple owners?

_0.content:
Good. So `users.json` currently has project membership implicitly (it's in the project's config directory). The new model separates that — `projects.json` holds the project-to-user mappings, and `users.json` stays as a user registry without project info.

Let me update the spec and then we can figure out where `projects.json` lives. It probably belongs outside any single project since it maps across projects — maybe at the server level?

@title Multi-User Collaboration — Design Spec
@argument
Design specification for the first multi-user collaboration features in Gossamer: invitation, roles, pending edits, and notification.
@status proto
@pub-time 2026-05-02T00:00Z
@category zzz-debug
@related 100, 101

## Overview

Allow one user (the owner) to invite others to share and collaborate on a project. This spec covers the MVP: roles, project/user data model, pending edit review, agent behavior, and summarization. The invitation flow and notification system are deferred — initial user/project mapping is done manually via an admin screen.

## Roles

Three roles, in order of privilege:

- **Owner.** Full control. Can accept or decline edits, make direct changes. One per project (for now).
- **Contributor.** Can participate in chat, ask the agent to propose edits. Cannot accept or decline edits — proposals go to the owner for review.
- **Observer.** Can see the chat and browse the project. Cannot speak in chat (no text input window shown). Cannot propose edits.

## Data model

Two JSON files serve as a minimal database.

### projects.json (server-level)

Lives outside any single project, since it maps across all projects on the server.

```json
[
  {
    "id": "SeedPods",
    "name": "SeedPods",
    "members": [
      { "userId": "FA2ABF2F-24EF-40A0-8D65-A0A4FFA3E4E3", "role": "owner" }
    ]
  }
]
```

Each project has an `id`, a display `name`, and a `members` array of `{ userId, role }` pairs. Roles are `owner`, `contributor`, or `observer`.

### users.json (server-level)

A user registry. No project membership — that lives in `projects.json`.

```json
[
  {
    "id": "FA2ABF2F-24EF-40A0-8D65-A0A4FFA3E4E3",
    "name": "Darakshan",
    "initials": "darak",
    "devices": [
      "F86E0D90-9B7A-4EDF-BCC0-70F721D036D9",
      "5E2DCE06-6BD4-4669-A4D9-177DEC7F31C7"
    ],
    "profile": "73 years old, experienced software architect, student of spiritual traditions and philosophy, founder of SeedPods"
  }
]
```

### Admin screen

A page in the admin interface allows the owner to:

- Add a user to a project with a role (by selecting from known users).
- Change a user's role.
- Remove a user from a project.

This replaces the full invitation flow for now. No notification badges, no accept/decline dialogue.

### Migration

The current `config/users.json` inside each project directory is retired. Its data moves to the server-level `users.json` and `projects.json`. The per-project file can remain as a read-only cache if needed for offline use, but the server-level files are authoritative.

## Project switching

Once a user has access to more than one project, they need a way to switch. Switching changes both the chat pane and the browse pane. The UI for this is TBD but could be a project selector in the settings pane or a swipe gesture.

## Pending edits

When a contributor asks the agent to make a change, the agent proposes the edit as a chip. But only the owner can accept or decline it.

- The chip enters a **pending** state. The conversation continues — it does not block.
- The contributor sees their proposal marked "waiting for owner."
- The agent knows the proposal is pending and does not treat it as applied.
- The owner can review pending edits in the browse pane. The proposed change appears as a highlighted diff overlay on the rendered subject, with accept/decline buttons in context.
- A **"next pending"** button on the chip lets the owner cycle through all pending edits.
- This is analogous to document review in Word or Google Docs, except the owner sees the change as a reader would, not as raw markup.

## Agent behavior in multi-user chat

- The agent can see all participants and their roles.
- **Subject detection:** The agent watches the conversation and matches it against subject arguments. When confident, it surfaces a relevant subject via the status line.
- **Hand-raising:** The agent can signal it has something to contribute via the status line, without interrupting.
- **Status line:** A single line the agent can write to at any time — subject detection, hand-raising, pending-edit count, reactions, emoji. Participants can react with emoji back.
- **Mute modes:** The group can toggle between lightweight mode (subject detection only) and full mode (agent follows conversation and can contribute). Muting saves cost.
- The group trains the agent over time. Ignored contributions are signal.

## Minutes (server-triggered summarization)

The server runs a configurable timer (default 30 minutes) after the last message in any user's current chat. When it fires, the server sends the agent a message asking it to summarize the conversation as a summary chip.

The summary captures three things:

- **What was established.** Things the group now holds that they didn't before.
- **What's open.** Things raised but not resolved.
- **What's next.** Anything anyone committed to doing.

These categories are domain-agnostic. The same mechanism fires when context is getting full. One feature, two triggers, same output. The summary is stored as a chip and persists across sessions.

## Notification system (deferred)

The full notification system is deferred to a later phase. It would include:

- Red dot on the lozenge for pending invitations and unread items.
- App badge when any notification is active.
- Settings sidebar highlighting the section wanting attention.
- Lozenge hides when keyboard is active, reappears when keyboard dismisses.
- Invitation flow: owner provides email, invitee gets badge, accept/decline/cancel dialogue.

## Open questions

- Where exactly does `projects.json` live on the server filesystem?
- What happens to a contributor's pending edits if they are removed from the project?
- How does the "next pending" button order the edits — chronological, by subject, by contributor?
- Should the agent carry context from previous group sessions via stored minutes, or start fresh each time?
- Can there be multiple owners?

@title Multi-User Collaboration — Design Spec
@argument
Design specification for the first multi-user collaboration features in Gossamer: invitation, roles, pending edits, and notification.
@status proto
@pub-time 2026-05-02T00:00Z
@category zzz-debug
@related 100, 101

## Overview

Allow one user (the owner) to invite others to share and collaborate on a project. This spec covers the MVP: invitation flow, roles, pending edit review, and notification indicators.

## Roles

Three roles, in order of privilege:

- **Owner.** Full control. Can invite and remove users, accept or decline edits, make direct changes. One per project.
- **Contributor.** Can participate in chat, ask the agent to propose edits. Cannot accept or decline edits — proposals go to the owner for review.
- **Observer.** Can see the chat and browse the project. Cannot speak in chat (no text input window shown). Cannot propose edits.

## Invitation flow

1. Owner opens Settings and provides the email address of an existing Gossamer user, plus a role (contributor or observer).
2. The system rejects the invitation if the email does not belong to a known user.
3. The invitee receives a notification badge on the app. Following it leads to a dialogue with three buttons: **Accept**, **Decline**, **Cancel**.
4. On accept, the invitee is added to an internal table for the project (`config/users.json` or equivalent).
5. Once a user has more than one project, they need a simple way to switch projects. Switching changes both the chat pane and the browse pane.

## Pending edits

When a contributor asks the agent to make a change, the agent proposes the edit as a chip. But only the owner can accept or decline it.

- The chip enters a **pending** state. The conversation continues — it does not block.
- The contributor sees their proposal marked "waiting for owner."
- The agent knows the proposal is pending and does not treat it as applied.
- The owner can review pending edits in the browse pane. The proposed change appears as a highlighted diff overlay on the rendered subject, with accept/decline buttons in context.
- A **"next pending"** button on the chip lets the owner cycle through all pending edits.
- This is analogous to document review in Word or Google Docs, except the owner sees the change as a reader would, not as raw markup.

## Agent behavior in multi-user chat

- The agent can see all participants and their roles.
- **Subject detection:** The agent watches the conversation and matches it against subject arguments. When confident, it surfaces a relevant subject in a non-interrupting way (status line or subtle announcement).
- **Hand-raising:** The agent can signal it has something to contribute via the status line, without interrupting the conversation.
- **Status line:** A single line the agent can write to at any time. Used for subject detection, hand-raising, pending-edit status ("1 pending edit — waiting for owner"), reactions, or any lightweight communication. Participants can react with emoji.
- **Mute modes:** The group can toggle between lightweight mode (subject detection only, cheap) and full mode (agent follows the conversation and can raise its hand). Muting saves cost.
- The group trains the agent over time. Ignored contributions are signal.

## Notification system

Notifications use the existing lozenge (the swipe-pane indicator with dots and labels).

- **Red dot:** A dot in the lozenge turns red when the corresponding pane has a notification. Settings pane: pending invitation. Chat pane: unread messages or pending edits.
- **App badge:** If any dot is red, the app itself gets a badge.
- **Settings sidebar:** The settings pane has a navigation sidebar. The specific section wanting attention (e.g. "Invitations") is highlighted red.
- **Lozenge visibility:** The lozenge hides when the keyboard is active (the user already knows they're in Chat). It reappears when the keyboard dismisses. The lozenge and keyboard naturally trade the same screen real estate.

## Minutes (server-triggered summarization)

The server runs a configurable timer (default 30 minutes) after the last message in any user's current chat. When it fires, the server sends the agent a message asking it to summarize the conversation as a summary chip.

The summary captures three things:

- **What was established.** Things the group now holds that they didn't before.
- **What's open.** Things raised but not resolved.
- **What's next.** Anything anyone committed to doing.

These categories are domain-agnostic — they work for engineers, writers, families, or any group.

The same mechanism fires when context is getting full. One feature, two triggers, same output. The summary is stored as a chip in the conversation and persists across sessions.

## Open questions

- How does the owner remove a user from a project?
- Can the owner promote an observer to contributor or vice versa?
- What happens to a contributor's pending edits if they are removed from the project?
- Should the agent carry context from previous group sessions via stored minutes, or start fresh each time?
- How does the "next pending" button order the edits — chronological, by subject, by contributor?
- What is the data model for the internal project membership table?

Allow one user (the owner) to invite others to share and collaborate on a project. This spec covers the MVP: roles, project/user data model, pending edit review, agent behavior, and summarization. The invitation flow and notification system are deferred — initial user/project mapping is done manually via an admin screen.

## Roles

Three roles, in order of privilege:

- **Owner.** Full control. Can accept or decline edits, make direct changes. One per project (for now).
- **Contributor.** Can participate in chat, ask the agent to propose edits. Cannot accept or decline edits — proposals go to the owner for review.
- **Observer.** Can see the chat and browse the project. Cannot speak in chat (no text input window shown). Cannot propose edits.

## Data model

Two JSON files serve as a minimal database. Both live in the root of shared data and will eventually be replaced by a single SQLite database.

### projects.json

```json
[
  {
    "id": "SeedPods",
    "name": "SeedPods",
    "members": [
      { "userId": "FA2ABF2F-24EF-40A0-8D65-A0A4FFA3E4E3", "role": "owner" }
    ]
  }
]
```

Each project has an `id`, a display `name`, and a `members` array of `{ userId, role }` pairs. Roles are `owner`, `contributor`, or `observer`.

### users.json

A user registry. No project membership — that lives in `projects.json`.

```json
[
  {
    "id": "FA2ABF2F-24EF-40A0-8D65-A0A4FFA3E4E3",
    "name": "Darakshan",
    "initials": "darak",
    "devices": [
      "F86E0D90-9B7A-4EDF-BCC0-70F721D036D9",
      "5E2DCE06-6BD4-4669-A4D9-177DEC7F31C7"
    ],
    "profile": "73 years old, experienced software architect, student of spiritual traditions and philosophy, founder of SeedPods"
  }
]
```

### Admin screen

A page in the admin interface allows the owner to:

- Add a user to a project with a role (by selecting from known users).
- Change a user's role.
- Remove a user from a project.

This replaces the full invitation flow for now. No notification badges, no accept/decline dialogue.

### Migration

The current `config/users.json` inside each project directory is retired. Its data moves to the server-level `users.json` and `projects.json`. The per-project file can remain as a read-only cache if needed for offline use, but the server-level files are authoritative.

## Project switching

Once a user has access to more than one project, they need a way to switch. Switching changes both the chat pane and the browse pane. The UI for this is TBD but could be a project selector in the settings pane or a swipe gesture.

## Pending edits

When a contributor asks the agent to make a change, the agent proposes the edit as a chip. But only the owner can accept or decline it.

- The chip enters a **pending** state. The conversation continues — it does not block.
- The contributor sees their proposal marked "waiting for owner."
- The agent knows the proposal is pending and does not treat it as applied.
- The owner can review pending edits in the browse pane. The proposed change appears as a highlighted diff overlay on the rendered subject, with accept/decline buttons in context.
- A **"next pending"** button on the chip lets the owner cycle through all pending edits.
- This is analogous to document review in Word or Google Docs, except the owner sees the change as a reader would, not as raw markup.

## Agent behavior in multi-user chat

- The agent can see all participants and their roles.
- **Subject detection:** The agent watches the conversation and matches it against subject arguments. When confident, it surfaces a relevant subject via the status line.
- **Hand-raising:** The agent can signal it has something to contribute via the status line, without interrupting.
- **Status line:** A single line the agent can write to at any time — subject detection, hand-raising, pending-edit count, reactions, emoji. Participants can react with emoji back.
- **Mute modes:** The group can toggle between lightweight mode (subject detection only) and full mode (agent follows conversation and can contribute). Muting saves cost.
- The group trains the agent over time. Ignored contributions are signal.

## Minutes (server-triggered summarization)

The server runs a configurable timer (default 30 minutes) after the last message in any user's current chat. When it fires, the server sends the agent a message asking it to summarize the conversation as a summary chip.

The summary captures three things:

- **What was established.** Things the group now holds that they didn't before.
- **What's open.** Things raised but not resolved.
- **What's next.** Anything anyone committed to doing.

These categories are domain-agnostic. The same mechanism fires when context is getting full. One feature, two triggers, same output. The summary is stored as a chip and persists across sessions.

## Notification system (deferred)

The full notification system is deferred to a later phase. It would include:

- Red dot on the lozenge for pending invitations and unread items.
- App badge when any notification is active.
- Settings sidebar highlighting the section wanting attention.
- Lozenge hides when keyboard is active, reappears when keyboard dismisses.
- Invitation flow: owner provides email, invitee gets badge, accept/decline/cancel dialogue.

## Open questions

- What happens to a contributor's pending edits if they are removed from the project?
- How does the "next pending" button order the edits — chronological, by subject, by contributor?
- Should the agent carry context from previous group sessions via stored minutes, or start fresh each time?
- Can there be multiple owners?

@title Multi-User Collaboration — Design Spec
@argument
Design specification for the first multi-user collaboration features in Gossamer: invitation, roles, pending edits, and notification.
@status proto
@pub-time 2026-05-02T00:00Z
@category zzz-debug
@related 100, 101

## Overview

Allow one user (the owner) to invite others to share and collaborate on a project. This spec covers the MVP: invitation flow, roles, pending edit review, and notification indicators.

## Roles

Three roles, in order of privilege:

- **Owner.** Full control. Can invite and remove users, accept or decline edits, make direct changes. One per project.
- **Contributor.** Can participate in chat, ask the agent to propose edits. Cannot accept or decline edits — proposals go to the owner for review.
- **Observer.** Can see the chat and browse the project. Cannot speak in chat (no text input window shown). Cannot propose edits.

## Invitation flow

1. Owner opens Settings and provides the email address of an existing Gossamer user, plus a role (contributor or observer).
2. The system rejects the invitation if the email does not belong to a known user.
3. The invitee receives a notification badge on the app. Following it leads to a dialogue with three buttons: **Accept**, **Decline**, **Cancel**.
4. On accept, the invitee is added to an internal table for the project (`config/users.json` or equivalent).
5. Once a user has more than one project, they need a simple way to switch projects. Switching changes both the chat pane and the browse pane.

## Pending edits

When a contributor asks the agent to make a change, the agent proposes the edit as a chip. But only the owner can accept or decline it.

- The chip enters a **pending** state. The conversation continues — it does not block.
- The contributor sees their proposal marked "waiting for owner."
- The agent knows the proposal is pending and does not treat it as applied.
- The owner can review pending edits in the browse pane. The proposed change appears as a highlighted diff overlay on the rendered subject, with accept/decline buttons in context.
- A **"next pending"** button on the chip lets the owner cycle through all pending edits.
- This is analogous to document review in Word or Google Docs, except the owner sees the change as a reader would, not as raw markup.

## Agent behavior in multi-user chat

- The agent can see all participants and their roles.
- **Subject detection:** The agent watches the conversation and matches it against subject arguments. When confident, it surfaces a relevant subject in a non-interrupting way (status line or subtle announcement).
- **Hand-raising:** The agent can signal it has something to contribute via the status line, without interrupting the conversation.
- **Status line:** A single line the agent can write to at any time. Used for subject detection, hand-raising, pending-edit status ("1 pending edit — waiting for owner"), reactions, or any lightweight communication. Participants can react with emoji.
- **Mute modes:** The group can toggle between lightweight mode (subject detection only, cheap) and full mode (agent follows the conversation and can raise its hand). Muting saves cost.
- The group trains the agent over time. Ignored contributions are signal.

## Notification system

Notifications use the existing lozenge (the swipe-pane indicator with dots and labels).

- **Red dot:** A dot in the lozenge turns red when the corresponding pane has a notification. Settings pane: pending invitation. Chat pane: unread messages or pending edits.
- **App badge:** If any dot is red, the app itself gets a badge.
- **Settings sidebar:** The settings pane has a navigation sidebar. The specific section wanting attention (e.g. "Invitations") is highlighted red.
- **Lozenge visibility:** The lozenge hides when the keyboard is active (the user already knows they're in Chat). It reappears when the keyboard dismisses. The lozenge and keyboard naturally trade the same screen real estate.

## Minutes (server-triggered summarization)

The server runs a configurable timer (default 30 minutes) after the last message in any user's current chat. When it fires, the server sends the agent a message asking it to summarize the conversation as a summary chip.

The summary captures three things:

- **What was established.** Things the group now holds that they didn't before.
- **What's open.** Things raised but not resolved.
- **What's next.** Anything anyone committed to doing.

These categories are domain-agnostic — they work for engineers, writers, families, or any group.

The same mechanism fires when context is getting full. One feature, two triggers, same output. The summary is stored as a chip in the conversation and persists across sessions.

## Open questions

- How does the owner remove a user from a project?
- Can the owner promote an observer to contributor or vice versa?
- What happens to a contributor's pending edits if they are removed from the project?
- Should the agent carry context from previous group sessions via stored minutes, or start fresh each time?
- How does the "next pending" button order the edits — chronological, by subject, by contributor?
- What is the data model for the internal project membership table?
